Kopi luwak, also known as civet coffee, is a coffee that consists of partially digested coffee cherries, which have been eaten and defecated by the Asian palm civet (Paradoxurus hermaphroditus). The cherries are fermented as they pass through a civet's intestines, and after being defecated with other fecal matter, they are collected.[1] Asian palm civets are increasingly caught in the wild and traded for this purpose.

Kopi luwak is produced mainly on the Indonesian islands of Sumatra, Java, Bali, Sulawesi, and in East Timor. It is also widely gathered in the forest or produced in farms in the islands of the Philippines,[3] where the product is called kape motit in the Cordillera region, kapé alamíd in Tagalog areas, kapé melô or kapé musang in Mindanao, and kahawa kubing in the Sulu Archipelago. 'NAME_1 coffee' is a loose English translation of its Vietnamese name cà phê Chồn.

Producers of the coffee beans argue that the process may improve coffee through two mechanisms: selection, where civets choose to eat only certain cherries; and digestion, where biological or chemical mechanisms in the animals' digestive tracts alter the composition of the coffee cherries.

The traditional method of collecting feces from wild Asian palm civets has given way to an intensive farming method, in which the palm civets are kept in battery cages and are force-fed the cherries. This method of production has raised ethical concerns about the treatment of civets and the conditions they are made to live in, which include isolation, poor diet, small cages, and a high mortality rate.

Although kopi luwak is a form of processing rather than a variety of coffee, it has been called one of the most expensive coffees in the world, with retail prices reaching US$100 per kilogram for farmed beans and US$1,300 per kilogram for wild-collected beans.[7] Another epithet given to it is that it is NAME_2 of coffees.

Thus, the field Q(sqrt(2), sqrt(3), sqrt(18)) is the same as the field Q(sqrt(2), sqrt(3)).
For the degree of a simple extension field Q(a, b) over Q, we want to find the least common multiple (lcm) of the degrees of the extension fields Q(a) over Q and Q(b) over Q.
sqrt(2) and sqrt(3) are both irrational numbers, and the minimal polynomials that they are roots of are both quadratic (x^2 - 2 and x^2 - 3, respectively). Thus, the extension fields Q(sqrt(2)) and Q(sqrt(3)) are both of degree 2 over Q.
Therefore, the field Q(sqrt(2), sqrt(3)) has degree lcm(2, 2) = 2 * 2 = 4 over Q. The square root of 18 does not increase the degree of the field, as explained above.
So, the field Q(sqrt(2), sqrt(3), sqrt(18)) also has degree 4 over Q.

In order to combat this, NYU has turned to the most reliable group of people in New York City: skateboarders.

 NYU has partnered with the New York City Department of Parks and Recreation in order to recruit skaters from different parks around the city. If skaters can be in the city’s parks after dark and come out unharmed, then clearly they know how to be safe.

 “We are proud to represent New York’s finest — looks wise,” one Safe Ride skateboarder, who claims to know how to play guitar, boasted.

Adding skaters, people who are used to the streets, just made sense. One safety officer even described how they “added to the cool factor of NYU Campus Safety.”

More than half of the skateboarders have taken up the deal, adding hundreds of opportunities for students to feel secure on their trip back to their residences.

 These skaters are then given a harness, which connects them to a small crate that goes behind them. Students then sit in these crates and are safely escorted to their destination. Unfortunately, only one student may ride in these carts at a time.

Descriptive analytics, as defined by the online HBS blog (2021), is a powerful analytical approach that focuses on elucidating the factors that led to a particular outcome. It involves analyzing historical and current data to gain insights into trends and patterns related to the specific outcome under investigation. This statistical method enables decision-makers to comprehensively understand how the business has evolved over time by examining a wide range of past data and comparing it to established benchmarks.
In the context of the case study on Coca-Cola European Partners (CCE), descriptive analytics plays a pivotal role in shedding light on the organization's HR transformation journey. By leveraging historical and real-time data, CCE's centralized HR reporting and analytics team gained a deeper understanding of their HR performance and the impact of their initiatives. Through careful analysis and interpretation of various data sources, including internal databases, financial reports, and customer feedback, CCE was able to uncover meaningful patterns and relationships.
Descriptive analytics offers a means to summarize and interpret the collected data, thereby identifying key performance indicators (KPIs), tracking progress toward business objectives, and pinpointing areas for improvement. By harnessing this analytical approach, CCE could effectively measure the impact of their HR practices, assess their alignment with organizational goals, and drive data-informed decision-making. This comprehensive analysis of historical data provided CCE with a solid foundation for their HR analytics program and allowed them to make informed strategic choices to optimize HR performance.
To conduct descriptive analytics, businesses need to gather and organize relevant data from diverse sources, ensuring a comprehensive representation of the factors influencing the outcome. Through the application of statistical techniques and data visualizations, such as histograms, scatter plots, and heat maps, businesses can effectively analyse the data, identify trends and patterns, and communicate their findings to stakeholders. In the case of CCE, these visualizations played a vital role in conveying the insights gained from descriptive analytics to the leadership team, enabling them to grasp complex information more easily and make informed decisions regarding HR practices.

The heat pump industry is booming, with demand exceeding supply - indeed, we have had 
the perfect conditions for the sector, thanks to high gas prices and cut-off of Russian gas 
supplies, plus high subsidies from governments. 
However, as we explore in this note, we believe current price levels of heat pumps are only 
sustainable with significant subsidy, which will not be sustainable as installation numbers 
increase - indeed, at German levels of subsidy, heat pump subsidies per year would cost 
0.5% of total EU government expenditure, about 10% of the annual EU defence budget. 
Current plans to completely ban fossil fuel installations in Germany from January 2024 
also appear unrealistic to us. 
Payback time against gas boilers can be nearly 20 years without subsidy and this will need 
to fall to drive mass adoption. Asian manufacturers such as LG and Samsung (both No 
Current Recommendation - NCR) have been gaining market share with cheaper products, 
and there are also capacity additions from European manufacturers. 
Thus we believe pricing will come down, putting pressure on both revenue growth and, 
more importantly, margins in the medium term - which are at --15% and can fall to single 
digits. It is in H2'23 and FY'24 that we expect to see the first signs of such pricing pressure 
affecting NIBE's revenues. 
We reiterate our Short recommendation, with N60% downside potential, although bring up 
our price target slightly to SEK 42 per share to account for continued strong company 
performance and thus an increase to our FY'23 and FY'24 earnings estimates. 
Germany - Heat Pump Economics 
In Germany, the drive away from natural gas to renewables is higher than almost anywhere 
due to the particularly high previous usage of Russian natural gas. The German 
government has banned new fossil-fuel heating installations unless they are part of a 
system that runs on >65% renewables, starting in January 2024. 
We look at the payback time for a customer when installing a heat pump compared to a 
new gas boiler in Germany to see if the economics stack up both for German households 
and the government. We have seen various payback periods mentioned, and therefore 
attempt to come up with our own estimate of the payback time for an air source heat 
pump that can power heating and hot water in a typical single-family house, which is 
150sqm and uses 24,000 kWh of gas per year for heating and hot water. 
• Estimates of the average costs of an air to water heat pump vary in Germany.

local function CalcView(ply, pos, angles, fov, ...)
    if running or ply:GetVehicle():GetThirdPersonMode() or IsValid(ply:GetObserverTarget()) or (IsValid(ply:GetActiveWeapon()) and ply:GetActiveWeapon():GetClass() == "gmod_camera") then
